text: The laparoscopic view shows the right ureter with visible peristalsis and the sacral pulmonary region. The torsion is observed at the level of the infundibulopelvic ligament. The small developing uterus is visible in the midline, alongside a tartate ovarian cyst with a twisted pedicle.

The surgeon identifies the left developing ovary and left ureter, noting visible peristalsis. The pediatric patient's thin peritoneum allows clear visualization of structures, including a large dermoid cyst on the ovary. The surgeon begins the procedure by untwisting the torqued ovary, noting no gangrene or color change. The round ligament is held to stabilize the ovary, and the harmonic device is used to incise the outer ovarian cortex to access the cyst.

The surgeon uses a 28-centimeter Maryland dissector to separate the inner cystic layer from the outer ovarian cortex during laparoscopy. The jaw of the Maryland is opened to create a dissection plane for enucleation. An Endo Catch bag with a metal ring is prepared to contain the cyst for removal, ensuring stability during the procedure.

The surgeon uses an 8 mm endo catch with a metal ring to capture the cyst, which has moved into the cul-de-sac. The endo bag is opened to create space for the cyst, and the ovarian ligament is held to manipulate the ovary during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully drops the cyst into the endobag, avoiding contact with the fallopian tube to prevent injury. An assistant lifts the cyst while the surgeon punctures it slightly, using suction irrigation to remove some cystic content. The cyst wall is held steady with a grasper to facilitate further manipulation.

The surgeon punctures the distended cyst, releasing fatty content that is aspirated using suction. The cyst is then held with a grasper or Maryland forceps for easier manipulation. The left hand holds the cyst while the right hand separates the ovarian cortex, requiring ambidextrous coordination to alternate holding and pushing the outer cortex for effective enucleation.

The surgeon performs enucleation of the cyst from the ovary within the endo bag to minimize spillage. Initially using an atraumatic grasper, the surgeon switches to two Maryland dissectors for better grip as the cystic wall is carefully separated from the ovarian tissue.

The surgeon continues the dissection, separating the cyst from the outer ovarian cortex, revealing a clear dissection plane. The cyst is being carefully extracted, with some fatty content attempting to escape, but efforts are made to keep it contained within the endobag.

The surgeon completes the separation of the cyst from the ovary, placing it into the endo bag. The outer cortex is dropped away, and the cystic content is secured within the bag. The endo bag's mouth is closed using its built-in suture, and the metal ring is cut. The assistant holds the bag separately while the surgeon uses ovum forceps to puncture and remove the cystic content. The telescope provides a view inside the endo bag during this process.

The surgeon inspects the contents of the endo bag using a telescope, observing fatty content and ensuring no residual dermoid tissue remains. Thorough lavage is performed with suction irrigation to clean the area and prevent hematoma formation. The surgeon checks for any bleeding or oozing, using the open jaw of a Maryland or bipolar OB dissector to desiccate and coagulate any potential bleeders.

The surgical field is thoroughly irrigated and suctioned, ensuring no residual cystic content or bleeding remains. The area is clean following the final lavage, completing the procedure for the right-sided approximately six-centimeter dermoid cyst.
